content.problemStatement:
**This is the hard version of the problem. The only difference is that, in this version, **Ki≤1018K_i \leq 10^{18}Ki​≤1018**.**

There’s a tree having NNN nodes, out of which, some nodes are **energetic**.

Initially, energy of all the nodes is 000. In the ithi^{th}ith second, every **energetic** node will make the energy of itself and all its neighbours **increase** by iii.

You need to answer QQQ queries. For the ithi^{th}ith query, you are given three integers:


XiX_iXi​ TiT_iTi​ KiK_iKi​ : Find the **minimum** number of seconds after which, there are **at least** TiT_iTi​ nodes with energy **not less than** KiK_iKi​ on the path from node 111 to node XiX_iXi​. If it is not possible to have **at least** TiT_iTi​ nodes with energy **not less than** KiK_iKi​ on the path from node 111 to node XiX_iXi​ after any amount of time, print −1-1−1 instead.

Input Format

The first line contains an integer NNN — the number of nodes.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers, the array AAA denoting whether the nodes are energetic. If Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1, it means that the ithi^{th}ith node is **energetic**, otherwise the ithi^{th}ith node is **not energetic**.
The next N−1N-1N−1 lines describe the edges. The ithi^{th}ith of these N−1N-1N−1 lines contains two space-separated integers UiU_iUi​ and ViV_iVi​, denoting an edge between UiU_iUi​ and ViV_iVi​.
The next line contains an integer QQQ — the number of queries.
The next QQQ lines contain three space-separated integers Xi,Ti,KiX_i,T_i,K_iXi​,Ti​,Ki​, the query.


Output Format
For each query, output on a new line: the **minimum** number of seconds after which, there are **at least** TiT_iTi​ nodes with energy **not less than** KiK_iKi​ on the path from node 111 to node XiX_iXi​.

If it is not possible to have **at least** TiT_iTi​ nodes with energy **not less than** KiK_iKi​ on the path from node 111 to node XiX_iXi​ after any amount of time, print −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: 1 ≤ Ti ≤ length of the path from 1 to Xi
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer N — the number of nodes in the tree.
The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, where Ai = 1 if node i is energetic and Ai = 0 otherwise.
Each of the next N‑1 lines contains two integers Ui Vi denoting an undirected edge between nodes Ui and Vi.
The next line contains an integer Q — the number of queries.
Each of the next Q lines contains three integers Xi Ti Ki.
For each query you have to output the minimum number of seconds after which at least Ti nodes on the simple path from node 1 (the root) to node Xi have energy not less than Ki. If it never becomes possible, output -1.
content.outputFormat: For each query, output a single line containing the answer described above.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Query 111: No matter how long, there will not be at least 333 nodes having energy not less than 111 on the path from node 111 to node 777. Thus, the answer is −1-1−1.
Query 222: In the 1st1^{st}1st second, the energy of nodes 1,3,1,3,1,3, and 666 becomes 2,3,2,3,2,3, and 222 respectively. Thus, there are at least 333 nodes with energy not less than 222.
Query 333: In the 2nd2^{nd}2nd second, the energy of nodes 1,4,1,4,1,4, and 777 becomes 6,3,6,3,6,3, and 000 respectively. Thus, there are at least 222 nodes with energy not less than 333.
Query 444: In the 3rd3^{rd}3rd second, the energy of nodes 1,3,1,3,1,3, and 555 is 12,18,12,18,12,18, and 666 respectively. Thus, there are at least 333 nodes with energy not less than 555.

solution.approach:
For a fixed number of seconds S the energy added by one energetic node equals S·(S+1)/2. Therefore the total energy of a node v after S seconds is
    energy[v] = C[v] * S·(S+1)/2,
where C[v] is the number of energetic nodes among v and its neighbours (this value never changes).
For a query (X,T,K) we need the smallest S such that at least T nodes on the path 1→X satisfy
    C[v] * S·(S+1)/2 ≥ K.
For a given S the condition can be rewritten as
    C[v] ≥ ceil( 2K / (S·(S+1)) ).
Thus for a fixed S we only have to count how many nodes on the root‑to‑X path have C[v] not smaller than a threshold value.
The answer S is monotone: if a certain S works, any larger S also works. Hence we can binary‑search S (0 … 2·10^9 is enough because S·(S+1)/2 exceeds 10^18 for S≈2·10^9).
To answer the count query fast we build a persistent segment tree (also called a functional segment tree). The tree stores frequencies of C‑values (range 0 … N). While performing a DFS from the root we create a new version for each node by inserting its C value into the version of its parent. The version of node X therefore represents the multiset of C values on the path 1→X.
Counting nodes with C ≥ threshold is a standard range‑sum query on the persistent segment tree: query(version[X], threshold, N).
Overall per query we perform O(log maxS) binary‑search steps, each step needs O(log N) time for the segment‑tree query, giving O(log maxS·log N) ≈ 60·16 operations. The preprocessing (building all versions) costs O(N·log N). Memory consumption is O(N·log N) nodes (≈ 6·10^5 for the given limits).
Edge cases:
* C[v] = 0 → the node can never reach a positive K.
* If even for S = 2·10^9 the count is < T, answer is -1.
* Ti may be larger than the number of nodes with C>0 on the path – also answer -1.
The algorithm works for the hard version because it never uses Ki directly in a loop; all arithmetic uses 128‑bit integers (unsigned long long in C/C++, Python int, Java long) to avoid overflow.
solution.timeComplexity: Preprocessing: O(N log N). Each query: O(log maxS · log N) ≈ O(60·log N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N log N) for the persistent segment‑tree nodes.
